name: Algorytm
classification_notes: The typeface features geometric alternate letterforms for characters such as 'a', 'f', 'g', 'r' and 't', pointing to a geometric sans construction, while the default set retains humanist traits that soften the overall character.
summary: A geometric sans from Interval Type spanning eight weights with extensive OpenType alternates and Cyrillic support.
description: The family runs from Extralight to Ultra with matching italics throughout, giving fourteen styles in total. Each weight ships with a substantial set of glyph alternates covering both Latin and Cyrillic characters, including one-storey and neutral 'a' variants, geometric cuts for 'f', 'r' and 't', and edgy or simplified options for 'g' and 'y'. The Cyrillic coverage includes alternates for К and Ж, indicating genuine attention to that script rather than a mechanical addition.
foundry_blurb: Algorytm is a geometric sans-serif offered in eight weights from Extralight to Ultra, each with an italic. The family includes a wide range of OpenType alternates allowing users to shift individual characters between humanist defaults and more geometric or simplified forms, and covers both Latin and Cyrillic scripts including specific alternates for Cyrillic letterforms.

name: Algorytm Clear
classification_notes: The extensive alternate character set — including one-storey 'a', geometric 'f', 'r' and 't', and simplified 'g' and 'y' — points firmly toward a geometric sans tradition, with the 'Clear' designation suggesting a neutral, legible variant of the broader Algorytm family.
summary: A geometric sans with a broad alternate character set covering Latin and Cyrillic, running across six weights with italics.
description: Algorytm Clear spans six weights from Extralight to Bold, each with a matching italic, and ships with an extensive suite of OpenType alternates that let designers toggle between humanist and strictly geometric letterforms. Character-level options include one-storey and neutral 'a' forms, geometric cuts of 'f', 'r' and 't', simplified and angular variants of 'g' and 'y', and dedicated Cyrillic alternates for К and Ж. The result is a workhorse geometric sans with enough flexibility to shift register between warm and clinical depending on the alternate set chosen.
foundry_blurb: Interval Type present Algorytm Clear as a versatile geometric sans available in six weights with italics, offering a rich set of character alternates — including multiple 'a', 'g', 'r', 't' and 'y' variants — as well as Cyrillic alternate forms, giving designers control over the typeface's overall character.

name: Algorytm Mono
classification_notes: A monospaced sans-serif with geometric construction, inspired by early American gothics. The fixed-width constraint places it firmly in the mono category, with a geometric sans underpinning.
summary: A monospaced geometric sans in six weights, drawn from early American gothic sources and extended with Cyrillic coverage and a rich set of stylistic alternates.
description: Part of the broader six-family Algorytm collection, the Mono variant applies a fixed-width grid to letterforms rooted in nineteenth-century American gothics such as Times Gothic and Inland Gothic. Six weights from Extralight to Bold are accompanied by alternates for 'a', 'g', 'y', 'u', 'G' and 'R', plus special characters including circled numbers and geometric parentheses. Latin Extended and Cyrillic scripts are both covered, with Cyrillic-specific alternates for К and Ж.
foundry_blurb: Interval Type describe Algorytm as a versatile collection of six sans-serif families inspired by early gothics including Times Gothic, Inland Gothic N6 and Gothic N2, infused with a modern aesthetic and a systematic approach prioritising multifunctionality and flexibility. Algorytm Mono is one of six families in the collection, which totals 51 styles across standard, clear, mono, flip, soft and sport variants, all supporting Extended Latin and Cyrillic.

name: Algorytm Sport
classification_notes: Algorytm Sport is a display variant of a sans-serif collection inspired by early American gothics, distinguished by inline letterforms that give it a sporting, energetic character placing it firmly in display territory.
summary: A single-weight inline display face drawn from the Algorytm collection, built around early American gothic letterforms with a sporting, high-energy character.
description: Part of a six-family collection rooted in early American gothics such as Times Gothic and Inland Gothic, Algorytm Sport distinguishes itself through lively inline lettering intended to add energy to layouts. The collection spans 51 styles across its six families, with Sport sitting at the expressive end alongside the reverse-contrast Algorytm Flip and the rounded Algorytm Soft. Coverage extends across Extended Latin and Cyrillic scripts.
foundry_blurb: Interval Type describe Algorytm Sport as the standout member of the Algorytm collection, embodying a vibrant and healthy appearance with lively inline letters designed to inject energy into layouts. The broader collection draws inspiration from early gothics including Times Gothic, Inland Gothic N6 and Gothic N2, combining a modern aesthetic with a systematic, multifunctional design approach across six families and 51 styles.

name: Algorytm Soft
classification_notes: Described as inspired by early gothic sans serifs such as Times Gothic and Inland Gothic, with a modern systematic approach; the rounded terminals place it firmly in the friendly grotesque subcategory.
summary: A rounded grotesque with early American gothic roots, offered in eight weights with an extensive set of stylistic alternates and Cyrillic support.
description: Part of the six-family Algorytm Collection from Interval Type, this variant applies softened, rounded corners to the underlying grotesque structure to produce a friendlier character. Eight weights from Extralight to Ultra are available, and the family ships with a generous palette of OpenType alternates covering single- and double-storey forms, geometric constructions and additional Cyrillic characters. Coverage extends across Extended Latin and Cyrillic scripts, making it a workable option for multilingual branding and interface projects.
foundry_blurb: Interval Type describe Algorytm Soft as the friendly, rounded-corner member of the Algorytm Collection, a six-family system inspired by early gothics such as Times Gothic and Inland Gothic. The collection totals 51 styles and is designed around multifunctionality, diversity and flexibility, with an abundance of alternates and special characters to suit a wide range of design scenarios.

name: Algorytm Flip
classification_notes: A grotesque sans-serif with reverse contrast, meaning stroke weight is heavier on horizontals than verticals, drawing on early American gothic types such as Times Gothic and Inland Gothic N6.
summary: A reverse-contrast companion within the Algorytm collection, drawing on early American gothic types and offering eight weights with an extensive set of character alternates.
description: Algorytm Flip sits within the broader six-family Algorytm collection, distinguished by its reversed stroke contrast where horizontals carry more weight than verticals. Eight weights run from Extralight to Ultra, and a rich alternate system covers single- and double-storey 'a', geometric variants for 'f', 'r' and 't', and Cyrillic alternates for К and Ж. Coverage extends across Extended Latin and Cyrillic scripts.
foundry_blurb: Interval Type describe Algorytm Flip as a reverse contrast companion within the Algorytm Collection, a group of six type families totalling 51 styles inspired by early gothics such as Times Gothic, Inland Gothic N6 and Gothic N2. The collection is built around a systematic approach prioritising multifunctionality, diversity and flexibility, with extensive alternates and OpenType features to serve a wide range of design scenarios.

name: Englisch
classification_notes: Inspired by 17th and 18th century English typography, particularly Fell Types and Caslon, with high vertical contrast and open forms; positioned explicitly as a display-exclusive family rather than a text typeface.
summary: A contemporary display serif drawing on 17th and 18th century English typefaces, particularly Caslon and the Fell Types, with deliberate eccentricity and high vertical contrast.
description: Six weights from Light to Black, each with a matching italic, make up a family designed for titles and logotypes rather than running text. The design takes cues from Caslon and the Fell Types without attempting a strict historical revival, instead pulling forward the open forms, vertical contrast and idiosyncratic rhythm of that period into a more modern register. Works across print and web wherever a headline face with period character is needed.
foundry_blurb: Interval Type describe Englisch as drawing on the typography of 17th and 18th century England, evoking historic typefaces such as the Fell Types and Caslon. Rather than a strict revival, it offers a contemporary interpretation that embraces eccentricity, vertical contrast and open forms, combining traditional aesthetics with modern touches and a playful rhythm. The family spans 6 weights and 12 styles, intended for impactful titles and logos across print and web.

name: FactorA Variable
classification_notes: The family's clean, modular construction with optional rounded lowercase and stylistic alternates places it firmly in the geometric sans tradition, with a variable format spanning weight and width axes.
summary: A variable geometric sans from Interval Type spanning seven weights, four width grades and matching italics, with stylistic alternates including a rounded lowercase and a 'Funk It Up' character set.
description: The family runs across seven weights from Ultralight to Black, each paired with an italic, and four width variants numbered 35, 65 and 100 alongside the default. Stylistic alternates give designers the choice between neutral and expressive versions of the 'f' and 't', a rounded lowercase, a slashed zero and a more characterful 'Funk It Up' set. The variable format packages the full range into a single file, making it practical for responsive or multi-context work.
foundry_blurb: Interval Type present FactorA Variable as a large-scale geometric sans family with a variable font offering full control over weight and width, complemented by OpenType alternates that allow the typeface to shift between neutral functionality and more expressive character.

name: Factor B
classification_notes: Described explicitly as a geometric grotesk drawing conceptual inspiration from Futura and Johnston Sans, with a rational approach and geometrically constructed alternates in the Altiverse set.
summary: A geometric grotesque with Futura and Johnston Sans in its lineage, offered in six weights with a secondary 'Altiverse' set of geometrically constructed alternates for display use.
description: Six weights from Light to Black give Factor B a workable range for both text and display settings, while the Altiverse alternate set adds geometric personality suited to logotypes and headlines. Caps and ascender heights are intentionally varied rather than strictly uniform, softening the rigidity typical of pure geometric grotesques. Extended Latin and Cyrillic coverage makes it viable for multilingual branding and web projects.
foundry_blurb: Interval Type describe Factor B as a functional and dependable geometric grotesk drawing conceptual inspiration from Futura and its alternates, synthesising the essence of early 20th-century sans-serif designs including Johnston Sans. The default character set offers a neutral, highly legible aesthetic, while the Altiverse set provides geometrically constructed alternates for added personality in titles and logos. The family supports Extended Latin and Cyrillic scripts and is suited to branding, web design, signage and more.

name: Factor A Mono
classification_notes: A monospaced companion to Factor A, itself a geometric grotesque; the fixed-width construction and geometric skeleton place it firmly in the monospaced category with geometric sans characteristics.
summary: A monospaced companion to Factor A, bringing the geometric grotesque's warmth and alternate glyph system to a fixed-width format across five weights.
description: Built as a counterpart to the Factor A family, this monospaced cut retains the geometric grotesk foundation while introducing the same alternate character sets, including rounded lowercase forms, a neutralised 'f' and 't', and a 'funk it up' set for display use. Five weights run from Ultralight to Bold, with no italics. Coverage extends across Extended Latin and Cyrillic, making it suitable for branding, web and signage work where a fixed-width grid is required.
foundry_blurb: Factor A Mono is described by Interval Type as a monospaced companion to Factor A, a functional geometric grotesque synthesising early 20th-century sans serif design. The family features low contrast, matching cap and ascender heights, and multiple alternate sets for lowercase letters, enabling both neutral utility and expressive display use across branding, web and signage applications.

name: Oceanic
classification_notes: Oceanic draws on 18th and 19th-century fat face and Didone traditions (Bodoni, Scotch Roman), featuring strong upright contrast across text and poster weights. The text cuts reduce contrast for continuous reading, placing the family between display Didone and transitional serif territory.
summary: A high-contrast serif spanning text and poster grades, drawing on fat face and Scotch Roman traditions while introducing deliberate quirks in certain letterforms.
description: Built across six weights with matching italics, Oceanic moves from restrained text sizes to expressive poster grades. The text cuts use reduced contrast and a slightly condensed form for legibility at small sizes, while the Poster weight foregrounds unconventional details such as the biomorphic terminals on the 'c' and 'e' and the intentionally unsettling proportions of 'R' and 'K'. Italic alternates include descender swashes and ascender variants for additional decorative flexibility. Coverage extends to Extended Latin and Cyrillic.
foundry_blurb: Interval Type describe Oceanic as drawing inspiration from the upright contrast of 18th and 19th-century fat faces like Bodoni, Canon and Scotch Romans, with whimsical details evoking prehistoric oceanic creatures in letters like 'c' and 'e', and capital 'R' and 'K' designed to create a sense of slight discomfort. The text family is described as sober, with reduced contrast and a slightly condensed form suited to continuous reading, while the display variant foregrounds the family's quirks.

name: Oceanic Text
classification_notes: Draws from 18th and 19th-century fat faces such as Bodoni and Scotch Romans, adapted with upright contrast and reduced display quirks for continuous text use. The text-optimised cut places it firmly in the transitional serif tradition.
summary: A text-optimised serif drawing from fat-face and Scotch Roman traditions, tamed for continuous reading with reduced contrast and subtly eccentric letterforms.
description: The Text family tones down the expressive quirks of Oceanic Display, reducing contrast and condensing the proportions for comfortable small-size setting. Upright contrast borrowed from Bodoni and Scotch Roman models gives it a period character, while details like the prehistoric-creature 'c' and 'e' add personality without disrupting the reading flow. Ten styles across five weights from Regular to Extrabold cover Latin Extended and Cyrillic, with swash and alternate options in the italic cuts.
foundry_blurb: Interval Type describe Oceanic as drawing inspiration from the upright contrast of 18th and 19th-century fat faces such as Bodoni, Canon and Scotch Romans. The Text variant reduces that contrast and condenses the form for small-size and continuous text use, aiming for neutrality and elegance while retaining enough personality to avoid becoming mundane. Unconventional details such as the creature-like 'c' and 'e', and deliberately unsettling capital 'R' and 'K', are carried over from the Display cut in a more restrained form.

name: Oceanic Text Mono
classification_notes: A fixed-width typeface with upright contrast derived from 18th and 19th-century fat faces such as Bodoni and Scotch Roman, placing it firmly in the monospaced category with strong serif heritage.
summary: A fixed-width serif in four weights, drawing its contrast and idiosyncratic details from 18th and 19th-century fat faces such as Bodoni and Scotch Roman.
description: Part of the broader Oceanic family, the Mono variant carries over the upright contrast and quirky detailing of the Display and Text cuts into a fixed-width structure suited to coding, tabular data and technical documents. Characters like the 'c' and 'e' retain their creature-like open terminals, and the capital 'R' and 'K' are deliberately conspicuous, lending personality to an otherwise functional format. Four weights from Regular to Bold cover Extended Latin and Cyrillic.
foundry_blurb: Interval Type describe Oceanic Mono as preserving the idiosyncratic charm of the Display and Text families within a fixed-width structure. It draws from 18th and 19th-century fat faces including Bodoni, Canon and Scotch Roman, incorporating whimsical details such as the 'c' and 'e' resembling prehistoric oceanic creatures. Designed for coding, tabular data and technical documents, it aims to balance readability, historical influence and practical alignment requirements.

name: Oceanic Grotesk
classification_notes: The foundry describes it as drawing on mid-20th-century modernist typography with neutrality and simplicity as core values, placing it squarely in the grotesque tradition.
summary: A mid-20th-century modernist grotesque designed as a sans companion to the Oceanic serif families, sharing identical vertical metrics for mixed-setting use.
description: Built to sit alongside Oceanic and Oceanic Text, this grotesque takes a neutral, modernist stance rather than echoing the high-contrast fat face character of its serif counterparts. Six weights run from Light to Poster with matching italics across 12 styles, and a variable font is included in the full family purchase. Extended Latin and Cyrillic coverage makes it broadly serviceable for European and Eastern European work.
foundry_blurb: Interval Type describe Oceanic Grotesk as a sans companion to the Oceanic serif families, designed around mid-20th-century modernist principles of neutrality and simplicity. It shares identical vertical metrics with Oceanic Text for use in continuous text or titles, and is available in six weights with matching italics plus a variable font format.

name: Oceanic Grotesk Compact
classification_notes: Self-described as a mid-20th-century modernist grotesque, guided by neutrality and simplicity, designed as a sans-serif companion to a high-contrast serif family.
summary: A compact grotesque designed as a sans-serif companion to the Oceanic serif families, drawing on mid-20th-century modernist typography with an emphasis on neutrality.
description: Built to sit alongside Oceanic Text and share its vertical metrics, the family runs six weights from Light to Poster with matching italics, totalling twelve styles plus a variable font. Where the Oceanic serifs reference 18th and 19th-century fat faces and Bodoni-style contrast, this grotesque takes a cooler, more restrained modernist position. Extended Latin and Cyrillic coverage broadens its reach for multilingual publishing and branding work.
foundry_blurb: Interval Type describe Oceanic Grotesk Compact as a trustworthy, versatile workhorse guided by neutrality and simplicity, crafted to complement the Oceanic serif families without replicating their letter designs. The family shares identical vertical metrics with Oceanic Text for effortless pairing in continuous text or titles, and is available in a variable font format alongside twelve static styles covering Extended Latin and Cyrillic scripts.

name: Oceanic Grotesk Condensed
classification_notes: A mid-20th-century modernist grotesque, explicitly designed around neutrality and simplicity as a sans companion to a serif family with Bodoni and Scotch Roman influences.
summary: A condensed grotesque drawn as a sans companion to the Oceanic serif families, covering six weights from Light to Poster with matching italics and a variable font format.
description: Designed to sit alongside the Oceanic and Oceanic Text serif families, the condensed grotesque shares identical vertical metrics with Oceanic Text for clean mixing in running text or display settings. The design takes mid-20th-century modernist grotesque conventions as its reference point, keeping contrast low and construction simple. Twelve styles span Light to Poster, all with matching italics, and a variable font is included for desktop and web use.
foundry_blurb: Interval Type describe Oceanic Grotesk Condensed as a trustworthy and versatile workhorse family guided by neutrality and simplicity, conceived as a sans-serif companion to the Oceanic serif families. Where the serifs echo 18th and 19th-century fat faces in the vein of Bodoni and Scotch Romans, the grotesque draws on a mid-20th-century modernist approach. The family shares vertical metrics with Oceanic Text, offers six weights and twelve styles including a Poster weight, and is available in variable font format with Extended Latin and Cyrillic coverage.

name: Oceanic Gothic
classification_notes: Inspired by early sans-serifs of the late 19th and early 20th centuries, Oceanic Gothic sits firmly in the grotesque tradition with its display-oriented character and period-influenced letterforms.
summary: A grotesque sans companion to the Oceanic serif families, drawing on late 19th and early 20th-century sans-serif models across six weights with matching italics and variable font support.
description: Designed as a counterpart to the Oceanic and Oceanic Text serif families, it borrows individual letter characteristics from Oceanic Display to maintain a shared typographic personality. Six weights run from Light to Poster, each with an italic, and the full family is also available as a variable font. Script coverage spans Extended Latin and Cyrillic, making it suited to a broad range of editorial and display work.
foundry_blurb: Interval Type describe Oceanic Gothic as a sans-serif counterpart to their Oceanic serif families, conceived to echo distinctive letterforms from Oceanic Display and create a unified typographic personality. They note its roots in early sans-serif typefaces from the late 19th to early 20th centuries, and position it as a display-led design that also works at text sizes, available in 6 weights and 12 styles including italics and as a variable font.

name: Rooftop
classification_notes: A contemporary grotesque sans spanning five widths from Ultra Condensed to Extended, with no visible stylistic quirks that would push it into humanist or geometric territory.
summary: A large grotesque sans family spanning six widths and eight weights, built for flexible deployment across print and screen contexts.
description: With five distinct width subfamilies running from Ultra Condensed through to Extended, the family totals well over eighty styles, each accompanied by an italic. The weight range covers Thin to Extrabold throughout every width, giving designers consistent tools across tight newspaper columns and broad display work alike. Its neutral grotesque character keeps it versatile rather than distinctive.
foundry_blurb: Interval Type offer Rooftop as a comprehensive grotesque family available across six widths — Ultra Condensed, Condensed, Compact, Regular, Wide and Extended — each in eight weights with corresponding italics.

name: Rooftop Mono
classification_notes: A monospaced typeface drawing directly from early 20th-century American grotesks, with squarish curves and a humanist construction approach that places it firmly in the grotesque mono subcategory.
summary: A monospaced grotesque with squarish curves and a humanist structure drawn from early American grotesque sources such as Lining Gothic and Gothic No. 2.
description: Rooftop Mono takes its cues from Barnhart Bros. and Spindler's Lining Gothic and Palmer & Ray's Gothic No. 2, applying their proportions to a fixed-width format. Slightly squarish curves and a blunt treatment of letters like f, t, r, g and y give it a utilitarian, tech-inflected character without abandoning legibility at small sizes. Three weights cover Regular, Medium and Bold, with coverage across Extended Latin and Cyrillic.
foundry_blurb: Interval Type describe Rooftop Mono as inspired by early 20th-century American grotesks, citing Lining Gothic of Barnhart Bros. and Spindler and Gothic No. 2 of Palmer & Ray as key references. They note its squarish curves as a somewhat forgotten approach that lends the face a sturdier, techier quality, complemented by a brutal construction of certain letters. The family is designed for legibility across all sizes in both print and web contexts.

name: Riegraf
classification_notes: Riegraf uses square slab serifs with a geometric construction influenced by grotesque sans-serifs, blending high-contrast letterforms with low-contrast numerals and currencies in a style the foundry calls a Geometric Fusion Serif.
summary: A geometric slab with high-contrast letterforms and square serifs, designed as a companion to Interval Type's grotesque family Riegla.
description: Riegraf takes the construction logic of a geometric grotesque and applies it to a slab serif framework, pairing high-contrast letters with low-contrast numerals that read almost as sans-serif. The result is a 10-style family spanning Light to Black with matching italics, offering alternate uppercase and lowercase characters in italic cuts. Extended Latin and Cyrillic coverage makes it viable across a broad range of European languages.
foundry_blurb: Interval Type describe Riegraf as a Geometric Fusion Serif born from the idea of making a serif that feels like a geometric sans-serif grotesk while retaining the elegance of serif letterforms. Simple square slab serifs and high-contrast letters sit alongside low-contrast numerals and currencies that recall a sans-serif, creating what they call a truly unique mixed design. It was designed as a companion to Riegla, a non-neutral grotesk also available from the foundry, with both families intended to work together.

name: Riegla
classification_notes: Described explicitly as a non-neutral grotesk drawing from 19th-century sans-serif designs such as Figgins Sans and Breite Grotesk, with upright contrast reminiscent of pre-rationalisation grotesques rather than the neutralised Swiss tradition.
summary: A contrasted grotesque with 19th-century roots, offering three sets of stylistic alternates and coverage across Extended Latin and Cyrillic.
description: Drawing from early sans-serif models such as Figgins Sans and Breite Grotesk, Riegla retains the contrast and character of pre-rationalisation grotesques rather than smoothing them into neutral utility. Ten styles span Light to Black with matching italics, and three stylistic alternate sets, labelled Jazz, Classic and individual glyph swaps, give designers control over its personality. Designed as a companion to the fusion serif family Riegraf, both sharing formal commonalities for mixed-family typesetting.
foundry_blurb: Interval Type describe Riegla as a multifunctional non-neutral grotesk inspired by 19th-century sans-serif designs including Figgins Sans, Breite Grotesk and various Gothics of that period, with emphasis on contrast and distinctiveness from the pre-rationalisation era. The design also carries references to simplified upright-contrast slab serifs, and is packed with stylistic alternates for versatility at both small text sizes and in display settings. It was conceived as a companion to Riegraf, a fusion serif family also available from Interval Type.

name: Stravinsky
classification_notes: Stravinsky fuses Didot-style vertical contrast and squarish counters with a contemporary grotesque sans-serif structure, making it a display-oriented hybrid that defies standard classification. The high contrast and experimental character place it firmly in the display category.
summary: A display typeface that grafts Didot-era vertical contrast and squarish counters onto a grotesque sans structure, yielding something deliberately unstable in character.
description: Six weights from Extralight to Extrabold give it range across headline and display contexts, while OpenType alternates including optional Didot serifs and Cyrillic alternates allow the designer to dial up or down its period references. Extended Latin and Cyrillic script support broadens its reach. It can read as traditional or conspicuously contemporary depending on how it is paired and which alternates are activated.
foundry_blurb: Interval Type describe Stravinsky as fusing 18th-century Didot vertical contrast and squarish counters with the contemporary grotesque sans-serif form. They characterise it as slightly chaotic and experimental, relying on a hidden internal order. Suited to highlighting, teasing and direct statement, it can read as traditional or as a risky fresh thing depending on how it is used.
